steps.title: Read the third sentence as a broad claim
steps.content: The third (underlined) sentence says, "But evolutionary links between predators and their prey can persist across centuries and continents." This is a sweeping generalization about predator-prey relationships, stated as a universal principle rather than as a fact about any particular species.

steps.title: See how surrounding sentences relate to it
steps.content: The sentences before set up a separation: mimosa evolved with B. terrenus in East Asia, then moved to North America in 1785 without the beetle. The sentences after deliver the specific case that illustrates the principle: when B. terrenus arrived around 2001, it attacked 93% of the trees within a year. The third sentence is the general rule; the mimosa/B. terrenus story is the example.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It states the hypothesis that Chang and colleagues had set out to investigate using mimosa trees and B. terrenus.’: the text never says Chang set out to test this idea; the beetle's arrival was incidental, not a planned experiment. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It presents a generalization that is exemplified by the discussion of the mimosa trees and B. terrenus.’: matches exactly — broad rule about persistent predator-prey links, then the mimosa case as the example. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘It provides context that clarifies why the species mentioned spread to new locations.’: the sentence says nothing about why the trees or beetles spread; humans introduced them. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘It offers an alternative explanation for the findings of Chang and colleagues.’: it is not an alternative explanation — it is the principle the findings confirm. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
